coredata.dc:title: N-doped, silver, and cerium co-doped carbon quantum dots based sensor for detection of Hg2+ and captopril
coredata.dc:creator: Dewangan, Lakshita
Chawre, Yogyata
Korram, Jyoti
Karbhal, Indrapal
Nagwanshi, Rekha
Jain, Vishal
Satnami, Manmohan L.
coredata.dc:description: A stable carbon quantum dots doped with nitrogen (N-CQDs), co-doped with silver (N/Ag-CQDs), and co-doped with cerium N/Ce-CQDs were synthesized using hydrothermal method. As-synthesized N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs showed high quantum yield compared to N-CQDs. These carbon quantum dots were used as a probe for the detection of mercury and captopril. The fluorescence quenching (turn-off) of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs was occured with the addition of Hg2+ ion. On the other hand, captopril showed fluorescence recovery (turn-on) of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs which are quenched by Hg2+ ion. The fluorescence recovery of CQDs is due to the high affinity of thiol group of captopril towards Hg2+ ion to form Hg-S bonds. On the basis of fluorescence quenching (turn-off), Hg2+ was determined with low limit of detection of 1.43 nM, 0.93 nM and 1.38 nM using N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs, respectively as fluorescence nanoprobes. The fluorescence turn-on of the CQDs has been applied for the detection of captopril with low limit of detection of 1.65 µM, 0.46 µM and 1.22 µM using N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs, and N/Ce-CQDs respectively. The developed sensing probe showed good sensitivity and high fluorescence efficiencies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CQDs
N/Ag-CQDs
N/Ce-CQDs
Fluorescence quenching
Fluorescence recovery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Hg2+ and captopril in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by DST-FIST No.SR/FST/CSI-259/2014(C) and UGC SAP No. F-540/7/DRS-II/2016 (SAP-I). The authors are grateful to acknowledge the Indian Institute of Technology Bombay for TEM analysis. We are also appreciative to Pt. Ravishankar Shukla University, Raipur for research scholar fellowship. We are thankful to National Center for Natural Resources, Pt. Ravishankar Shukla University, Raipur for FTIR analysis.

This work was supported by DST-FIST No.SR/FST/CSI-259/2014(C) and UGC SAP No. F-540/7/DRS-II/2016 (SAP-I). The authors are grateful to acknowledge the Indian Institute of Technology Bombay for TEM analysis. We are also appreciative to Pt. Ravishankar Shukla University, Raipur for research scholar fellowship. We are thankful to National Center for Natural Resources, Pt. Ravishankar Shukla University, Raipur for FTIR analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–visible spectra of (a) N-CQDs (b) N/Ag-CQDs, (c) N/Ce-CQDs and fluorescence spectra of (d) N-CQDs (e) N/Ag-CQDs (f) N/Ce-CQDs at different excitation wavelength.
The XPS spectra of (a) N -CQDs (b) C 1 s (c) N 1 s (d) O 1 s.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs in the presence of Hg2+ ion (b) plot of FI/FI ₒ vs [Hg2+] (inset linear plot) (c) Fluorescence spectra of N/Ag-CQDs in the presence of Hg2+ ion (d) plot of FI/FI ₒ vs [Hg2+] (inset linear plot) (e) Fluorescence spectra of N/Ce-CQDs in the presence of Hg2+ ion (f) plot of FI/FI ₒ vs [Hg2+] (inset linear plot) [linear equation for b, d &f is y = a + b*x ].

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs + Hg2+ in the presence of captopril (b) plot of FI/FI ₒ of N-CQDs vs [captopril] (c) Fluorescence spectra of N/Ag-CQDs + Hg2+ in the presence of captopril (d) plot of FI/FI ₒ of N/Ag-CQDs vs [captopril] (e) Fluorescence spectra of N/Ce-CQDs + Hg2+ in the presence of captopril (f) plot of FI/FI ₒ of N/Ce-CQDs vs [captopril] [linear equation for b, d &f is y = a + b*x ].
Schematic representation of precursor used for synthesis of carbon quantum dots and their luminescence behaviour in the presence of Hg2+ and captopril.
Optical properties, sizes, surface charge and elemental composition of CQDs.

head.ce:title: N-doped, silver, and cerium co-doped carbon quantum dots based sensor for detection of Hg2+ and captopril

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Studies in Chemistry, Pt. Ravishankar Shukla University, Raipur (C. G.) 492010, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Studies in Chemistry, Pt. Ravishankar Shukla University, Raipur (C. G.) 492010, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Govt. Madhav Science P. G. College, Ujjain (M.P.) 456010, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Govt. Madhav Science P. G. College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Govt. Madhav Science P. G. College, Ujjain (M.P.) 456010, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University Institute of Pharmacy, Pt. Ravishankar Shukla University, Raipur (C. G.) 492010, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University Institute of Pharmacy, Pt. Ravishankar Shukla University, Raipur (C. G.) 492010, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A stable carbon quantum dots doped with nitrogen (N-CQDs), co-doped with silver (N/Ag-CQDs), and co-doped with cerium N/Ce-CQDs were synthesized using hydrothermal method. As-synthesized N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs showed high quantum yield compared to N-CQDs. These carbon quantum dots were used as a probe for the detection of mercury and captopril. The fluorescence quenching (turn-off) of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs was occured with the addition of Hg2+ ion. On the other hand, captopril showed fluorescence recovery (turn-on) of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs which are quenched by Hg2+ ion. The fluorescence recovery of CQDs is due to the high affinity of thiol group of captopril towards Hg2+ ion to form Hg-S bonds. On the basis of fluorescence quenching (turn-off), Hg2+ was determined with low limit of detection of 1.43 nM, 0.93 nM and 1.38 nM using N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs, respectively as fluorescence nanoprobes. The fluorescence turn-on of the CQDs has been applied for the detection of captopril with low limit of detection of 1.65 µM, 0.46 µM and 1.22 µM using N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs, and N/Ce-CQDs respectively. The developed sensing probe showed good sensitivity and high fluorescence efficiencies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) luminescence efficiency was evaluated in a useful way like heteroatom doping [1–3]. Compared with undoped CQDs, non-metallic atom like nitrogen doping on CQDs decreases some surface defects, increases their optical properties and quantum yields. CQDs doped with non-metallic atom (N) in combination with either co-doping with metal (Ag+) and rare earth element (Ce3+) could provide very bright fluorescence with good surface passivation, resistance to photobleaching and ultra-high photoluminescence quantum yields because of excitation energy traps [4–7]. The photoluminescence mechanism of heteroatom CQDs or co-doped CQDs depends on energy traps and conjugated electronic structure. The co-dopant silver (Ag+) is used as biocompatible element with nitrogen passivated surface of CQDs, which enables the participation of lone pair electron of nitrogen for effectively enhancement of quantum yields [6,8–9]. On the other hand silver act as a strong lewis acid which has high affinity for nitrogen donor atom, that could result in formation of a stable complex between Ag-N by electron transfer mechanism and the outcome was successfully synthesized N/Ag-CQDs. In the N/Ce-CQDs, carboxyl group of CQDs can coordinate with Ce3+ in the interstitial state and provide more electrons for the CQDs. Cerium shows high stability in Ce3+ state, which protects the 4f energy level from the crystalline field and external chemical environment, and is used as an antioxidant for biomedical applications [10–14]. The applications of single heteroatom doped and co-doped CQDs have been reported for detection of drugs like daunorubicin [15], methimazole [16], gemcitabine [17], levodopa [18], cisplatin [19], and heavy metal ions like iron [20], arsenic [21], mercury [22], lead [23], organic pollutants [24], peroxides [25] and copper [26].

Mercury (Hg2+) is a toxic heavy metal pollutant and has become a serious problem worldwide because of its endanger impact on human health [27]. For the sake of human health, it causes serious damage to kidney, brain, endocrine system and central nervous system even at very low concentration. [28–29] Numerous analytical techniques such as colorimetric [30], fluoremetric [31], electrochemical [32] and surface enhanced raman spectroscopy [33] have already been developed for detection of Hg2+. Moreover, captopril functions as angiotensin converting enzyme inhibitor. The enzyme converts the angiotensin I to angiotensin II, which regulate blood pressure. Captopril is used as hypertension drug, and also prevents from strokes, heart attack and kidney problems. Captopril operates in human body to increase the blood and oxygen supply to the heart through the blocking the substances which tighten the blood vessels and make them relax [34–36]. Jiang et al. [37] developed the B-N codoped carbon quantum dots for captopril detection with limit of detection 0.03 µM/L using fluorescence method. As well as Paimard et al. [38] studied the 1,4-phenylene-N,N′-bis (O,O-diphenylphoramidate) (PDPP), l-cysteine capped CdS quantum dots (CdS-QDs) and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) for detection of captopril using electrochemical method. Recently, several methods have been developed for detection of captopril such as colorimetric [39], electrochemical [40], liquid chromatography mass spectrometry [41] and fluorescence [42] measurements. The methods so far reported are less sensitive, time-consuming, expensive and involve complex operating procedure. Present investigation reports dual purpose probe for detection of Hg2+ and captopril at high analytical sensitivity. This study aimed to initiate the development of nanoprobe for rapid and user-friendly detection of Hg2+ and captopril.

In this work, the non-metal, metal and rare-earth element doped CQDs have been synthesized successfully using hydrothermal method. As-synthesized CQDs were characterized through the UV–visible spectra, photoluminescence spectra, FTIR spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectra and TEM analysis. The synthesized N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs show chemical stability with increased active sites and decreased the defects on surface. These carbon quantum dots were used as fluorescence probe for detection of Hg2+ and captopril. The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs decreases in the presence of Hg2+ ions due to the greater affinity of Hg2+ ions towards the nitrogen to form non-fluorescent complex. Further, addition of captopril solution to the non-fluorescent CQD-Hg2+ complex leads to the fluorescence recovery. The existence of thiol group on captopril favours the Hg-S bond formation and detachment of Hg2+ from CQDs. The single heteroatoms and co-doped CQDs were found to enhance sensitivity and instigate the switch On-Off-On behaviour of the probe. These sensing systems provide a good analytical performance in the linear range of 1 to 5 nM with very low limit of detection (LOD) values of 1.43 nM, 0.93 nM and 1.38 nM, using N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs, respectively for detection of Hg2+. For captopril detection, linear range of 2 to 8 µM with LOD values of 1.65 µM, 0.46 µM and 1.22 µM, were obtained using N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs respectively. Therefore, the evolved probe is very sensitive, selective, simple and method is very fast for the detection of Hg2+ and captopril. The present investigation is summarized in scheme 1 .

For the synthesis of N-CQDs, 1 mL polyethyleneimine and 1.0 g citric acid were added into 15 mL MilliQ water. The solution mixture was transferred to Teflon coated autoclave and heated at 180 °C at for 10 h. For synthesis of N/Ag-CQDs, were synthesized using 1 mL ethylenediamine and 0.1 g AgNO3 and citric acid. The solution mixture was transferred to Teflon coated and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. For synthesis of N/Ce-CQDs, 1.68 g cerous nitrate hexahydrate and 0.8 g citric acid were added in 15 mL MilliQ water and the solution was stirred for 60 min. After 60 min, 1.5 g glycine was added in the solution mixture. The solution mixture was then transferred to Teflon coated autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling at room temperature, all three solution mixture was dialyzed for 24 h.

All the synthesized three different CQDs were used as probes for detection of Hg2+. In first system, 400 µL of N-CQDs, in second system 500 µL of N/Ag-CQDs, and in third system 500 µL of N/Ce-CQDs, were added in the PBS buffer (7.0 pH). The stock solution of mercury was prepared using MilliQ water. The different concentrations of Hg2+ were added in to all the three different probes (N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs) solution mixture. The fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs were recorded with excitation at 360 nm, 310 nm and 340 nm, respectively.

Various concentration of captopril from 10 to 60 µM was added in the PBS buffer (pH 7.0). The constant amount of CQDs (400 µL of N-CQDs, 500 µL of N/Ag-CQDs and 500 µL of N/Ce-CQDs) was added in solution mixture with prior addition of 400 nm Hg2+ ion. The fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs were recorded with excitation at 360 nm, 310 nm and 340 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Hg2+ and captopril in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the real samples analysis, pond water and river water samples were used to study the practical ability of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs. Aliquots of different concentrations of Hg2+ (1 nM, 3 nM and 5 nM) have been prepared by stocks solutions of Hg2+. Further Hg2+ was mixed with N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs, N/Ce-CQDs in the presence of real samples and the fluorescence was measured at definite excitation wavelength. For the captopril detection, 10 tablets were grounded into fine powder using mortal pestle and stocks solutions were prepared in the buffer solution. Further, 2 µM, 6 µM and 8 µM have been spiked to investigate the practical ability of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs, respectively.

N-CQDs shows the UV–visible absorption peak at 360 nm and considered that the trapping of excited state energy of the surface states is trapped, which can lead to strong fluorescence (Fig. 1 a). The N/Ag-CQDs shows the UV–visible absorption peak at 310 nm, and shoulder peak at 370 nm which attributed to n-π* transition from –CONH- bond. The n-π* transition is originated from the –CO- bond, and –NH bond is attributed to the red shift of this band (Fig. 1 b). In N/Ce-CQDs, the UV–visible absorption at 340 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition indicating the existence of aromatic structures in the CQDs (Fig. 1 c) [43–44]. In N/Ce-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity is enhanced due to the increase of electron density at the surface of the CQDs [45]. The N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs give bright blue photoluminescence (PL) with quantum yields of 26.6 %, 30.2 % and 29.4 %, respectively. The photoexcited electrons through the π-π* transitions were proposed to relax into either the sp2 energy levels or the defect states (here termed as the surface state) giving rise to blue PL. The FL spectra of CQDs excited at various excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 410 nm show red shift (Fig. 1 d-f), because of the surface defects on the surface of CQDs. The emission wavelength of CQDs depends on factor like ratio of heteroatom content and surface functional groups. The co-doping of nitrogen with metals improve the electronic properties and chemical reactivity of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs by providing large reactive groups (–OH, –COOH, -NC, –CN, –NH2) on the surface of CQDs. Also, emission spectra show red shifted behaviour due to lower electronegative atom present on surface of CQDs.

The TEM image of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs shows average diameter of 4.26 nm, 3.73 nm and 4.21 nm with the lattice fringes at 0.33 nm, 0.34 nm and 0.36 nm, respectively (Fig. S-1 a, b & c). The zeta potential value for N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs is obtained as 16.72 mV, −36.01 mV and −4.76 mV, respectively (Fig. S-1 d, e & f).

The FTIR spectra of all three CQDs confirm that the many functional groups are present on the surface to interact with analytes (Fig. S-2). In Fig. S-2 a, the bands at 1247.95 cm−1 and 1529.85 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibration of pyridinic-N group (CNC) and pyrrolic group (NH). The band at 1686.35 cm−1, 2842.87 cm−1 attributed to the N-CO group, CH group, respectively. The peaks at 2321.41 cm−1 confirm that the CN group is present on the surface of N-CQDs. The OH/NH group may be attributed to the band at 3233.24 cm−1 which proved that the surface of CQDs is successfully doped with nitrogen [46]. The Fig. S-2b shows the FTIR spectra of N/Ag-CQDs in which band at 1517.20 cm−1, 1378.99 cm−1 and 1666.05 cm−1 are respectively assigned to the stretching vibration of pyrrolic group (NH), CN group and N-CO amide group. The peak at 3625.06 is attributed to the hydroxyl group (OH). The peak at 3233.24 cm−1 is decreased, indicating that the N/Ag-CQDs are formed. Fig. S-2c confirmed that the larger reactive functional groups are present on the surface of N/Ce-CQDs. The band at 1380.15 cm−1, 1519.32 cm−1, and 1681.70 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibration of CN group, pyrrolic group (NH) and N-CO amide groups, respectively. The two peaks at 1134.27 cm−1 and 1279.63 cm−1 are attributed to COC group and pyridinic (CNC) group. The CN groups and OH/NH groups are considered to stretch at 2328.86 cm−1 and 3378.09 cm−1. The strong peak at 2328.86 cm−1 compared to N-CQDs has confirmed that the interaction between N and Ce is involved [47].

The elemental composition of N-CQDs is obtained as C (18.57 %), N (8.98 %) and O (72.46 %) exist on the surface of N-CQDs [48] Fig. 2 a display the distinct peaks of C, N, and O in XPS spectrum. The three peaks at 281.77 eV, 282.36 eV and 285.02 eV in C 1 s spectrum are assigned to CC/CC, CN and COC, C-OH respectively (Fig. 2 b). The distinguished peaks in N 1 s spectrum of N-CQDs at 395.66 eV, 396.67 eV, 398.34 eV and 399.23 eV are ascribed pyridinic-N (CNC), pyrrolic-N (C-NH), graphitic-N [N(C)3] an quaternary-N nature of doped nitrogen, respectively (Fig. 2 c). The incorporation of oxygen in N-CQDs as CO and C-OH, COC groups are assigned to the two peaks at 528.47 eV and 532.48 eV of O 1 s spectrum, respectively (Fig. 2 d). The XPS results reveal that the N-CQDs is synthesized successfully, and carbon quantum dots is doped with nitrogen.

The elemental composition of N/Ag-CQDs was identified as C (57.08 %), N (23.79 %), O (18.79 %) and Ag (0.34 %) using XPS measurement (Fig. S-3 a). The deconvolution of C 1 s spectrum gives three peaks at 281.69 eV, 283.128 eV and 284.93 eV which corresponds to CC/CC, CN and C-OH, COC, respectively (Fig. S-3b). The N 1 s spectrum displays four distinct peaks of Ag-N, pyridinic-N (CNC), pyrrolic-N (C-NH), graphitic-N (N(C)3) and oxidized NO species at 395.446 eV, 396.82 eV, 398.47 eV and 403.51 eV, respectively (Fig. S-3c). In O 1 s spectrum, the peak at 528.89 eV is assigned to Ag-O and CO (Fig. S-3 d). The Ag 3d spectrum shows two peaks at 373.8 eV and 367.98 eV corresponding to Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2, respectively (Fig. S-3 e). The obtained results indicated that the surface of CQDs is enriched with nitrogen, and oxygen containing groups [49].

The elemental composition of N/Ce-CQDs was obtained as C (54.62 %), N (16.0 %), O (28.16 %) and Ce (1.52 %) using XPS measurement (Fig. S-4 a). The high resolution spectrum of C 1 s is deconvoluted into three peaks at 282.08 eV, 283.41 eV and 285.822 eV which corresponds to CC/CC, CN and COC, C-OH, respectively (Fig. S-4b). The N 1 s spectrum displays four peaks at 397.86 eV, 399.01 eV, 400.18 eV and 403.39 eV corresponding to pyridinic-N (CNC), pyrrolic-N (C-NH) graphitic-N (N(C)3), an oxidized NO species, respectively (Fig. S-4c). The two peaks of O 1 s at 531.34 eV and 529.51 eV are assigned to COC, C-OH and COO–….…Ce3+, respectively (Fig. S-4 d). The Ce 3d spectrum shows the two strong peaks at 882.83 eV and 902.75 eV which corresponds to the Ce (III). The peak at 880.68 eV, 884.34 eV, 897.72 eV and 908.32 eV indicated the existence of Ce (IV) (Fig. S-4 e). The XPS data confirmed that the cerium, nitrogen, oxygen and carbon elements on the surface of CQDs.

In the N-CQDs, many N-centres like graphitic, pyridinic and pyrrolic are present, in which more abundant pyridinic, pyrrolic centre are located at edges of the CQDs [50]. It is very difficult to identify the effect of different N-centres on FL behaviour of N-CQDs because of lack of there are no experimental techniques to verify this fact. But, computational modelling can compliments the experiments; it can be used to study systems with known contents of individual dopants. The N/Ag-CQDs emission peaks shows strong red shift due to graphitic-N doping which is high compare to N-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs [51]. Sarkar et al. [52] reported that the functional theory graphitic-N doping leads to the red shift because it can reduces the HOMO-LUMO gap and reduces the energies of transitions with a strong frontier orbital components. The graphitic-N level in the N-CQDs is high compared to N/Ce-CQDs leading to the low HOMO-LUMO gap of N-CQDs. The HOMO-LUMO gap in the presence of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N are quite similar, but graphitic-N doping can reduces the gap. The pyrrolic-N atoms can enhances phtotoluminescence by generating more protonation process or aromatic structures, which facilitates radiative recombination in N/Ag-CQDs. In all the three synthesized CQDs (table S-1) the low levels of graphitic-N framework indicated that the proportion of N-elements inside the carbon cores is decreased, and pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N distribution on the edges are increased [53–54].

The co-doping of N-CQDs, with Ag and Ce showed photoluminescence emission at longer wavelength. The optical properties, sizes, surface charges and elemental composition of synthesized CQDs are summarized in the Table 1 . The red shift of photoluminescence can be explained through the measurement of HOMO-LUMO band gap in the CQDs. The energies of highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) have been calculated using cyclic voltammogram (Fig. S-5). Further, the band gaps of synthesized carbon quantum dots have been calculated using UV–visible absorption spectra. The reduction potential obtained by cyclic voltammogram for N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs is −0.24 eV, −0.28 eV and −0.37 eV, respectively. The cyclic voltammetry measurement was made using three electrodes where glassy carbon electrode is used as working electrode, platinum wire is used as counter electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode is used as reference electrode. The cyclic voltammetry was scanned between −1.0 to 1.0 with the 0.01 V scan rate. The obtained reduction potential value was applied in equation below [55–56,48]:
(1)
E
LUMO
= - e
E
red .
+
4.4
where, Ered is reduction potential, and ELUMO is the energy of lowest unoccupied molecular orbital. After the solving eq. (1), ELUMO values obtained are −4.16 eV, −4.13 eV and −4.03 eV for N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs, respectively. LUMO value is calculated using equation below:
(2)
E
HOMO
=
E
LUMO
-
E g
where, Eg is band gap, and EHOMO is highest unoccupied molecular orbital. The band gap for N-CQDs is 3.44 eV, N/Ag-CQDs is 4.12 eV and N/Ce-CQDs is 3.65 eV. The obtained EHOMO is −7.61 eV, −8.25 eV and −7.67 eV for NCQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs, respectively.

The fluorescence spectra of CQDs were recorded with excitation at 360 nm for N-CQDs, 310 nm for N/Ag-CQDs, and 340 nm for N/Ce-CQDs. The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs is decreased with increasing concentrations of Hg2+ from 10 to 400 nM (Fig. 3 a). This fluorescence quenching is because of formation of non-fluorescent complex between Hg2+ and N-CQDs through the coordination of nitrogen of N-CQDs. Mercury (Hg2+) ions may stimulate nitrogen and oxygen atoms of the surface of N-CQDs and electronic structure can changed and non-fluorescence recombination is formed. Fig. 3 b shows the linear relation in the concentration range from 10 to 100 nM with good correlation coefficient 0.9793. The fluorescence intensity linearly decreases with concentration, and shows very low detection limit (LOD) of 1.43 nM, and limit of quantification (LOQ) of 4.32 nM with correlation regression range in the 1 to 5 nM. The found detection limit have been compared with other previously reported method and presented in Table S-2. The FL intensity of N/Ag-CQDs is also decreased with the addition of Hg2+ from 10 to 500 nM (Fig. 3 c), and showed linear relation in the range from 10 to 100 nM with correlation coefficient 0.9728 (Fig. 3 d). Due to Ag-Hg amalgam formed on the surface of N/Ag-CQDs, and reduction of Hg2+ ions into Hg by N/Ag-CQDs, FL intensity is decreased.41 Moreover, the fluorescence quenching of N/Ce-CQDs is observed with increasing concentrations from 10 to 500 nM of Hg2+ ions (Fig. 3 e). A good linear relationship has been obtained between 10 and 100 nM with correlation coefficient 0.9731 (Fig. 3 f). This fluorescence quenching can be due to collective effect of coordination of Hg2+ through nitrogen and oxygen atom on the surface of N/Ce-CQDs. The fact that the radius of Ce3+ ions is 1.12 Å and Hg2+ ions is 1.14 Å, and therefore, Ce3+ can be easily displaced by Hg2+ ions to form non-radiation recombination [53]. Zeta potential values confirm that the N/Ce-CQDs and N/Ag-CQDs have negatively charged on the surface. Hence, electrostatic interaction may also involve with Hg2+ ions. The fluorescence quenching efficiency is 93.54 %, 94.15 % and 93.63 %, for N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N-Ce/CQDs, respectively. The low limit of detection i.e. 0.93 nM and 1.38 nM obtained (LOQ values are 2.19 nM and 4.16 nM) with correlation regression in the ranges of 1 to 5 nM have been using N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs probe, respectively. The obtained LOD and LOQ values are low or equal compared to previously reported method (Table S-2).

The fluorescence of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs has been studied using fluorescence lifetime measurement. The average lifetime value for N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs is 6.71 ns, 6.67 ns and 6.79 ns, respectively. Further the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs has been confirmed through fluorescence lifetime measurement. The fluorescence lifetime value of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs is decreased in the presence of Hg2+ is 5.01 ns, 4.78 ns and 5.41 ns, respectively (Fig. S-6). The given data also proved that the non radiative electron transfer from the excited state of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs to the d orbital of the Hg2+ ion. Hence, dynamic quenching is possible in between the N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs, N/Ce-CQDs and Hg2+.

The fluorescence recovery of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs has also been studied using captopril in presence of Hg2+ ions. The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs are continuously increases with increasing concentrations of captopril in presence of Hg2+ ions. The concentration ranges from 10 to 65 µM, 10 to 65 µM and 10 to 60 µM, have been added into buffer solution containing N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs, respectively. The non-metallic doped N-CQDs/Hg2+ give good fluorescence recovery percentage 92.65 % at concentrations ranges from 10 to 65 µM for captopril (Fig. 4 a). Fig. 4 b indicated that the linear relation ranges from 10 to 65 µM with correlation coefficient 0.9951 has been obtained (Fig. 4 b). Fig. 4 c shows the FL intensity of N/Ag-CQDs/Hg2+ in the presence of captopril, and Fig. 4 d shows linear relation from 10 to 65 µM with good correlation coefficient 0.9978. The concentration ranges from 10 to 60 µM is added to the N/Ce-CQDs and highest recovery percentages of 93.08 % is obtained (Fig. 4 e). The linear relation has been found in the ranges from 10 to 60 µM of captopril with correlation coefficient 0.9992 (Fig. 4 f). The obtained result shows that captopril contains thiol group which forms Hg-S bond in the recovery reaction. Hence, mercury ions show high bonding affinity towards captopril, and the fluorescence intensity of CQDs is recovered. The recovery of the fluorescence intensity depends on the concentrations of captopril. The obtained data shows low limit of detection of 1.641 µM, 0.454 µM and 1.217 µM, and correlation regression ranges from 2 to 8 µM, 2 to 8 µM and 2 to 8 µM for N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs, respectively. The obtained LOQ value is 4.97 µM, 1.377 µM and 3.659 µM, for N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs, respectively, which is low or equal compared to previously reported method (Table S-2).

The synthesized probes have been applied in real samples for detection of both Hg2+ ions and captopril. For the detection of Hg2+ ions in real samples, river water and pond water sample were used. Captopril was detected in commercially available tablets (Table S-3). The appropriate amount of Hg2+ ions were spiked into the probes N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs, with buffer solution. Further, fluorescence spectra were recorded in the presence of real samples. The concentration of Hg2+ were found as 1.08 to 5.19, 0.95 to 4.95 nM, 0.85 to 4.85, in river water and 1.18 to 5.19 nM, 1.15 to 5.16 nM, 0.92 to 4.92 nM, in pond water using N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs, respectively. The probe shows good RSD values less then < 2.24, <2.32, <1.94, for river water and < 5.19, <2.61, <4.92, for pond water using N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs, and N/Ce-CQDs, respectively. Moreover, practical ability of CQDs probes has been applied in tablets samples. Firstly, 10 tablets were grounded into fine powder using mortar pestle and the appropriate amount of fine powder was added to the PBS buffer solutions. The proper amount of captopril was spiked into probes solution then fluorescence spectra were recorded. The captopril found from 2.06 to 8.12 µM, 2.05 to 8.09 µM and 1.99 to 77.97 µM, using N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs, respectively. The probe gives good RSD value less than < 0.34, <2.0, and < 0.19 using N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs, respectively. Hence, on the basis of obtained results, it can be concluded that the synthesized probes show high potentiality towards practical ability.

The interference study of ions on the determination of Hg2+ ions is shown in Fig. 10. The experimental result confirms that the metal ions Zn2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, K+, Al3+, Co2+, Fe3+, Ni2+ and Pb2+ did not interfere the probe (Fig. S-7 a). To study the interfering effect the same concentrations of interferent and analytes are used Hg2+ (400 nM) and captopril (60 µM). Further, the effect of interfering analytes such as glutathione, l-cysteine, glucose, sucrose, ascorbic acid and uric acid has been studied for determination of captopril (Fig. S-7b). The obtained results confirm that co-existence of other analytes did not affect the fluorescence probe. On the selectivity studies, it is found that Hg2+ shows fluorescence quenching compared to other metal ions. Other metal ions like Zn2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, K+, Al3+, Co2+, Fe3+, Ni2+ and Pb2+ show slight fluorescence quenching effect, which could be negligible (Fig. S-7c). It is observed that the fluorescence of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs, N/Ce-CQDs, solution containing Hg2+ is recovered immediately in the presence of captopril. Other thiol compounds like glutathione, l-cysteine, and organic compound such as glucose, sucrose, ascorbic acid and uric acid, shows negligible fluorescence recovery of the probe (Fig. S-7 d). Therefore, developed probe exhibited high selectivity towards Hg2+ ions and captopril.

In this work, three different probes N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs, N/Ce-CQDs, have been synthesized successfully using hydrothermal approach. The comparison between single heteroatom doping and metal co-doping has been made. The metal atom co-doped N-CQDs shows high fluorescence efficiencies in double doped heteroatom doping, because Ag and Ce chelates with carboxyl groups of CQDs and preserve more carboxyl groups by chelation. Hence, transition metal (N/Ag-CQDs) and rare earth element (N/Ce-CQDs) in conjunction with nitrogen shows high passivation, which increases the photoluminescence quantum yields. The doping of rare earth element is difficult compare to transition metal. Both type of N/Ag-CQDs, N/Ce-CQDs doping induces high quantum yields and bright photoluminescence. Further a synthesized N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs, N/Ce-CQDs, were used for the detection of Hg2+ ion and captopril. The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs, N/Ce-CQDs, is decreased in the presence of Hg2+ ion, whereas FL intensity of N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs, N/Ce-CQDs, is increased with the addition of captopril in the presence of Hg2+ solution. The probes N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs, show low limit of detection is 1.43 nM, 0.93 nM, 1.38 nM, and 1.65 µM, 0.46 µM, 1.22 µM, for Hg2+ ions and captopril, respectively. Hence, this work establishes that the heteroatom co-doped carbon quantum dots show high sensitivity, bright photoluminescence, high quantum yields and good fluorescence efficiency.

Lakshita Dewangan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Yogyata Chawre: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Jyoti Korram: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Indrapal Karbhal: Conceptualization, Data curation, Visualization. Rekha Nagwanshi: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Vishal Jain: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Manmohan L. Satnami: Supervision, Conceptualization, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

The authors are grateful to acknowledge the Indian Institute of Technology Bombay for TEM analysis. We are also appreciative to Pt. Ravishankar Shukla University, Raipur for research scholar fellowship. We are thankful to National Center for Natural Resources, Pt. Ravishankar Shukla University, Raipur for FTIR analysis.

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots prepared with polyethyleneimine as both reducing agent and stabilizer for synthesis of Ag/CQDs composite for Hg2+ ions detection
